clues.clue: Abbreviated 'ch,' this foundation row of interlocking loops begins most projects.
The shortest, tightest basic stitch, abbreviated 'sc' in U.S. patterns, is this.
Taller and abbreviated 'dc' in U.S. terms, this stitch begins with one yarn over before inserting the hook.
Nearly flat and abbreviated 'sl st,' this move joins rounds or travels across without adding height.
Even taller than a double, this U.S. 'tr' stitch starts with two yarn overs.
This hooked implement, sized by millimeters and letters, is the craft's defining tool.
This count of stitches and rows per inch determines whether a finished piece comes out the intended size.
Yarn is graded into these thickness categories, from lace and fingering up through bulky.
This natural protein fiber shorn from sheep is a classic material, though acrylic is cheaper.
These small clips or rings are placed on the work to track counts and pattern positions.
This Japanese-derived technique produces small stuffed animals and figures.
This classic motif, worked outward in rounds and joined into blankets, shares its name with a grandmother.
This adjustable loop start, cinched shut after the first round, prevents a center hole in amigurumi.
Wetting or steaming a finished piece to set its final shape and dimensions is this process.
Crocheters jokingly use this amphibian-inspired term for ripping out stitches ('rip it, rip it').
Abbreviated 'yo,' this fundamental motion wraps the working strand around the hook.
The extra chains at a row's start that raise the working strand to the proper height are the 'turning' these.
An unfinished project, abbreviated WIP, is a 'work in' this.
Working two stitches together to lower the count, abbreviated 'dec,' is this.
U.S. and this nation's terms clash: the U.S. 'single crochet' equals their 'double crochet.'
Unlike knitting, which uses two needles, this craft is worked with just this many hooks.
This looped-fabric craft takes its name from the French word for 'hook.'
Worked on a long hook that holds many loops at once, this hybrid style is named for a North African country.
Covering public poles and statues in colorful yarn cozies is this guerrilla street-art movement.
During the 1840s Great Famine, fine thread crochet gave impoverished Irish workers this to survive.

final.clue: Yarn spun from the soft coat of this South American camelid, a relative of the llama, is prized by crafters.
